star-trail-calculator.beginner: Stars appear to move across the sky because the Earth rotates. If your shutter is open long enough, stars will streak into trails in your photo. The star trail calculator helps you find the maximum exposure time for pinpoint stars, or plan long exposures to create beautiful circular star trail arcs.

0.heading: How Stars Move
0.text: The Earth rotates 360° in approximately 23 hours and 56 minutes (a sidereal day), so stars move at about 15 arcseconds per second at the celestial equator. Stars near the poles (Polaris in the north) trace smaller circles and appear to move more slowly.

1.heading: The 500 Rule vs. NPF Rule
1.text: The classic "500 Rule" divides 500 by your effective focal length to estimate maximum exposure before trailing is visible — but it was designed for lower-resolution sensors. The NPF Rule accounts for pixel pitch, focal length, aperture, and declination, producing shorter and more accurate limits for modern high-resolution cameras.

2.heading: Stacking for Star Trails
2.text: For intentional star trails, photographers shoot many shorter exposures (typically 30s each) and stack them in software. This avoids noise buildup, allows for gap-free trails, and means a single bad frame doesn't ruin the entire shot.

0.label: Focal Length
0.description: Longer focal lengths magnify star movement, requiring shorter exposures for pinpoint stars. A 14mm lens can expose 5-10 times longer than a 200mm lens.

1.label: Sensor Resolution
1.description: Higher-resolution sensors reveal star trailing at shorter exposure times. The 500 Rule is too generous for modern 40MP+ cameras.

2.label: Latitude & Declination
2.description: Stars near the celestial poles move less per second. Pointing toward Polaris allows longer exposures before trails appear.

3.label: Stacking vs Single Exposure
3.description: For star trails, stacking many short exposures produces cleaner results than one extremely long exposure, and allows recovery from interruptions.

0.text: Use the NPF Rule instead of the 500 Rule for modern cameras. It accounts for sensor resolution and produces more accurate maximum exposure times, especially with high-megapixel bodies.

1.text: For star trail stacking, keep gaps between frames under 2-3 seconds using a wired remote or intervalometer. Longer gaps create dotted rather than smooth trails.

2.text: Include Polaris (or the South Celestial Pole) in your composition for dramatic concentric circle trails. Stars closer to the pole trace tighter arcs.

mode.definition: Choose "Sharp Stars" to calculate maximum exposure time for pinpoint stars, or "Star Trails" to plan exposure sequences for intentional trail effects.

focalLength.definition: The lens focal length in millimeters. Longer focal lengths magnify apparent star movement, requiring shorter individual exposures.

sensor.definition: The physical sensor dimensions. Combined with resolution, determines pixel pitch, which affects how quickly star trailing becomes visible.

resolution.definition: The sensor resolution in megapixels. Higher resolution cameras reveal star trailing sooner because each pixel covers a smaller angular area of sky.

aperture.definition: The lens opening. For astrophotography, use the widest aperture available (lowest f-number) to capture the most starlight in each frame.

latitude.definition: Your geographic latitude. Affects the apparent motion rate of stars — at higher latitudes, stars near the pole move more slowly across the frame.

exposurePerFrame.definition: The shutter speed for each individual frame in a star trail sequence. Typically 15-30 seconds to capture enough light while avoiding excess noise.

numberOfFrames.definition: How many individual exposures to take. More frames mean longer, more dramatic star trails. 120 frames at 30 seconds each produces one hour of trails.

gapBetweenFrames.definition: The time between exposures for the camera to save the file. Keep this as short as possible (1-3 seconds) to avoid dotted trails.

0.scenario: You are photographing the Milky Way with a 24mm lens on a full-frame 24MP camera. Using the 500 Rule, approximately how long can you expose before stars begin to trail?
0.hint: The 500 Rule: maximum seconds = 500 / focal length.
0.successMessage: Right! 500 / 24 = approximately 20 seconds. This is a good starting point, though the NPF Rule might suggest a slightly shorter time for critical sharpness.
0.failureMessage: Apply the 500 Rule: divide 500 by your focal length (24mm). That gives you approximately 20 seconds.

1.scenario: You want to photograph the Orion Nebula with a 200mm lens on a crop-sensor camera (1.5x crop factor). What is the approximate max exposure using the 500 Rule?
1.hint: With a crop sensor, multiply the focal length by the crop factor first, then apply the rule.
1.successMessage: Correct! The effective focal length is 200mm x 1.5 = 300mm. 500 / 300 = ~1.7 seconds. At telephoto lengths on crop sensors, you have very little time before trailing appears.
1.failureMessage: Remember to account for the crop factor: 200mm x 1.5 = 300mm effective. Then 500 / 300 gives about 1.7 seconds.

2.scenario: You want to create a 2-hour star trail photo. You plan to shoot 30-second exposures with 2-second gaps. How many frames will you need?
2.hint: Calculate how many 32-second intervals (30s exposure + 2s gap) fit into 2 hours (7200 seconds).
2.successMessage: Correct! 7200 seconds / 32 seconds per frame = 225 frames. You will get smooth, continuous trails across 2 hours of Earth rotation.
2.failureMessage: Two hours = 7200 seconds. Each frame cycle is 30s + 2s = 32s. Divide 7200 by 32 to get the number of frames needed.

3.scenario: Your friend uses a 50MP full-frame camera and followed the 500 Rule with a 20mm lens, getting a 25-second exposure. They complain that stars look like short streaks when zoomed in. Why?
3.hint: The 500 Rule was designed for older, lower-resolution sensors.
3.successMessage: Exactly! The 500 Rule is too generous for high-resolution sensors. A 50MP full-frame sensor has a pixel pitch of about 4.4 micrometers, and the NPF Rule would recommend roughly 10-12 seconds for truly sharp stars.
3.failureMessage: The 500 Rule was created when sensors had far fewer megapixels. With 50MP, each pixel covers a much smaller slice of sky, revealing star motion that was invisible on older cameras.

0.label: The lens was out of focus

1.label: The 500 Rule is too generous for high-resolution sensors

2.label: The camera was shaking in the wind

4.scenario: You want a 1-hour star trail image. Why do experienced astrophotographers stack many short exposures (e.g., 240 x 15s) instead of taking a single 1-hour exposure?
4.hint: Think about what happens to sensor noise, battery life, and recoverability during an extremely long single exposure.
4.successMessage: Correct! A single hour-long exposure accumulates massive thermal noise, drains the battery, and if anything goes wrong (vibration, dew, a car headlight) the entire shot is ruined. Stacking short frames keeps noise low, lets you remove bad frames, and is far more forgiving.
4.failureMessage: Long single exposures build up severe thermal noise and risk total loss from any interruption. Stacking short frames keeps noise manageable, lets you discard ruined frames, and produces cleaner results.

0.label: Stacking produces longer trails than a single exposure

1.label: Single exposures cannot record star movement

2.label: Stacking reduces noise, allows recovery from interruptions, and saves battery

3.label: There is no practical difference — it is purely personal preference
